From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Regular season](/w/index.php?title=Regular_season&redirect=no "Regular season"))

Season of a sports league or competition, generally a specific one year period

In an organized [sports league](/wiki/Sports_league "Sports league"), a typical **season** is the portion of one [year](/wiki/Year "Year") in which regulated games of the [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") are in session: for example, in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") the season lasts approximately from the last week of March to the last week of September.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In other [team sports](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport"), like [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") or [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), it is generally from August or September to May although in some countries – such as Northern Europe, North America or East Asia – the season for outdoor summer sports starts in the spring and finishes in autumn, mainly due to weather conditions encountered during the winter.

A year can often be broken up into several distinct sections (sometimes themselves called seasons). These are: a preseason, usually a series of [exhibition games](/wiki/Exhibition_games "Exhibition games") played for training purposes; a regular season, the main period of the league's competition; the [postseason](/wiki/Postseason "Postseason"), a playoff tournament played against the league's top teams to determine the league's champion; and the offseason, the time when there is no official competition.

## Preseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_(sports)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Preseason")]

Main article: [Exhibition game](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game")

In association football, many clubs tour and then play a series of [exhibition games](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game") for training purposes. Specifically in Brazil, teams play in [state championships](/wiki/State_football_leagues_in_Brazil "State football leagues in Brazil") before the start of the [Brazilian League](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A").

In baseball, many professional clubs go to spring camp and followed by an exhibition [spring training](/wiki/Spring_training "Spring training") season.

In [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football"), the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")'s [preseason](/wiki/NFL_preseason "NFL preseason") and the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League")'s preseaon are highly structured three– or two–game series of games, respectively, in which teams are afforded a larger roster limit and play games that do not count toward their records; they are used to evaluate and prepare talent for the upcoming regular season. In [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football"), particularly that played at the [FBS level](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision"), a true preseason is not played but many teams play a "[spring game](/wiki/Spring_game "Spring game")" which is an internal team practice structured like a game.

In the highest levels of professional [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), the preseason (November–December) consists of extensive period of training on and off the court (gym/fitness work as well as working on tennis-specific skills like for example improving the accuracy of serve).

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_(sports)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Regular season")]

In sport, the term "regular season" or "home-and-away season"[[2]](#cite_note-FortFizel2004-2) refers to the sport's [league competition](/wiki/Championship#League_system "Championship"). The regular season is usually similar to a [group tournament](/wiki/Tournament#Group_tournaments "Tournament") format: teams are divided into groups, conferences and/or divisions, and each club plays a set number of games against a set number of opponents. In most countries the league is played in a double round-robin format, where every team plays every other team twice, once at their home venue, and once away at the opposition's venue as visitors. The results over all games are accumulated and when every team has completed its full schedule of games, a winner is declared.

In North America, the scheduling is different. Rather than every team playing all others twice, teams usually play more games against local rivals than teams in other parts of the country. For example, the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") will play the [Los Angeles Clippers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Clippers "Los Angeles Clippers") (a team within their division, a subdivision of the conference) four times in a regular season, while both will only play the [Toronto Raptors](/wiki/Toronto_Raptors "Toronto Raptors"), who are in the opposite [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(NBA) "Eastern Conference (NBA)"), twice. Part of this is due to the vast geographic distances between some teams in North America—measured in a straight line, Los Angeles is 3,494 kilometres from Toronto, for instance—and a desire to limit travel expenses. In the scheduling system used in the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL"), it is possible for two teams to only meet every four years, and to only have 2 common opponents in a season. [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") has the most uneven schedules of all the four major North American sports. In MLB, the conferences are called leagues instead, but have exactly the same effect as [conferences](/wiki/Athletic_conference "Athletic conference") (as with all [North American major leagues](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada"), leagues, conferences, and division are not based on skill, but instead geography, history, and rivalries). Prior to scheduling changes in 2023, teams played 19 games against each of the teams in their own division each year but only played 20 games total against all of the teams in the other league. Because each of the inter-league match-ups was part of a 3-game series or a 2-game series, teams played no games at all against most teams from the other league. They played 6 of the 15 teams in the other league, a historically high number (until 1997, [interleague play](/wiki/Interleague_play "Interleague play") was limited to exhibition matches and the postseason [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), and thus MLB teams did not play the other league's teams at all). In 2023, teams played all interleague teams in a single season for the first time; teams now play a 3-game series against all teams, except their designated interleague rival with 4 games, totalling to 46 games (later increased to 48 games and a 6-game series with their interleague rival for the forthcoming 2025 season).

In Australia, the two largest football leagues, the [AFL](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") ([Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football")) and [NRL](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") ([rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league")), both grew out of competitions held within a single city (respectively Melbourne and Sydney) and only began expanding to the rest of the country when inexpensive air travel made a national league possible. These leagues use a single [table](/wiki/League_table "League table") instead of being split into divisions. The term "home and away season" is sometimes used instead of regular season.

Many football leagues in Latin America have a very different system. Because most Latin American countries never had a football cup competition, they instead split their season into two parts, typically known as the [Apertura and Clausura](/wiki/Apertura_and_Clausura "Apertura and Clausura") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") for "opening" and "closing"). Most countries that use this system, Argentina being one notable example, crown separate league champions for each part of the season, using only league play. A few others, such as Uruguay, crown one champion at the end of a playoff involving top teams from each half of the season. Mexico operates its Apertura and Clausura as separate competitions that both end in playoffs. Brazil has a different system, the season starts with the state championships in January (every Brazilian state has its own championship), these state championships ends in April. The [Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") itself starts in May and ends in early December, and is played in a double round-robin format in the same way as the European championships.

A system similar to the Apertura and Clausura developed independently in [Philippine professional basketball](/wiki/Philippine_Basketball_Association "Philippine Basketball Association"), with formerly two, now three tournaments (called "conferences") in one season, with each conference divided into an "elimination round" (the single round-robin group stage) and the playoffs in the North American sense. Winning the playoffs is the ultimate goal of every team for every conference; while there is no season championship, [winning all conferences within a single season](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(PBA) "Grand Slam (PBA)") is rare and has only happened five times since 1975, with the two most recent examples occurring in [1996](/wiki/1996_Alaska_Milkmen_season "1996 Alaska Milkmen season") and [2013–14](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_San_Mig_Super_Coffee_Mixers_season "2013–14 San Mig Super Coffee Mixers season"). The qualifying round and playoffs setup has permeated down to the local level and in most team sports, although seasons are not divided into conferences.

## Postseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_(sports)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Postseason")]

Main article: [Playoffs](/wiki/Playoffs "Playoffs")

Many sports leagues have playoffs or "finals" that occur after the regular season is complete. A subset of the teams enter into a playoff [tournament](/wiki/Tournament "Tournament"), usually a [knockout tournament](/wiki/Knockout_tournament "Knockout tournament"), generally a pre-determined number with better overall records (more wins, fewer losses) during the regular season. There are many variations used to determine the champion, the league's top prize. In many of these leagues, winning the league's top prize at the conclusion of the postseason is more important than winning the regular season. This includes the [major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") ([MLB's World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), the [NBA Finals](/wiki/NBA_Finals "NBA Finals"), the [NFL's Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), the [NHL's Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/Stanley_Cup_Final "Stanley Cup Final"), the [MLS Cup](/wiki/MLS_Cup "MLS Cup"), and the [CFL's Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup")), and the major Australian sports (BBL Grand Final, [NBL Grand Final](/wiki/NBL_Grand_Final "NBL Grand Final"), [A-League Grand Final](/wiki/A-League_Grand_Final "A-League Grand Final"), [AFL Grand Final](/wiki/AFL_Grand_Final "AFL Grand Final") and [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final")).

European leagues have also started holding playoffs after a double round-robin "regular season". The [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") started its [promotion playoffs](/wiki/Football_League_play-offs "Football League play-offs") in 1987, with the third up to the sixth-ranked teams participating for the final promotion berth (the two top teams are automatically promoted). Elsewhere, relegation playoffs are also held to determine which teams would be relegated to the lower leagues. One prominent top-level football league, the [Eredivisie](/wiki/Eredivisie "Eredivisie") of the Netherlands, uses two different playoffs—one for relegation purposes, and the other to determine one of the league's entrants in the following season's [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League"). In [Super League Greece](/wiki/Super_League_Greece "Super League Greece"), which currently has two places in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and three in the Europa League, the teams that finish second through fifth in the regular season enter a home-and-away "playoff" mini-league. Since one Europa League place is reserved for the country's cup winner, only three of the four teams are guaranteed a place in the next season's European competitions (unless both the cup winner and runner-up are already qualified for Europe by other means). The playoff determines the country's second Champions League participant, and the points at which the two or three Europa League entrants join that competition. Conversely, some leagues like the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") do not hold a postseason, and therefore these leagues' champions and relegation are instead based on the regular season records.

Although rugby union did not become professional until 1995, that sport has a long history of playoffs, primarily in France and the Southern Hemisphere. The French national championship, now known as [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14"), staged a championship final in its first season of 1892, first used more than one round of playoffs in 1893, and has continuously operated a playoff system (except during the two World Wars) since 1899. South Africa's [Currie Cup](/wiki/Currie_Cup "Currie Cup") has determined its champions by playoffs since 1968, and New Zealand's [National Provincial Championship](/wiki/National_Provincial_Championship_(1976%E2%80%932005) "National Provincial Championship (1976–2005)"), the top level of which is now known as the [Mitre 10 Cup](/wiki/Mitre_10_Cup "Mitre 10 Cup"), has used playoffs since its creation in 1976. Argentina's [Nacional de Clubes](/wiki/Nacional_de_Clubes "Nacional de Clubes") has determined its champion by playoffs since its inception in 1993. Currently, two separate competitions feed into the Nacional, the [Torneo de la URBA](/wiki/Torneo_de_la_URBA "Torneo de la URBA") (for Buenos Aires clubs, held since 1899) and [Torneo del Interior](/wiki/Torneo_del_Interior "Torneo del Interior") (for the rest of the country); both use playoffs to determine their champions. [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby"), involving regional franchises from Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa and national franchises in Argentina and Japan, has used playoffs to determine its champions since its creation as Super 12 in 1996.

By contrast, other European countries were slow to adopt playoffs in rugby union. The [English Premiership](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby") only began playoffs in [1999–2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "1999–2000 English Premiership (rugby union)"), and did not use them to determine the league champion until [2002–03](/wiki/2002-03_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "2002-03 English Premiership (rugby union)"). The Celtic League, now known as [Pro14](/wiki/Pro14 "Pro14"), resisted a playoff system even longer; its champions were determined solely by league play from its inception in [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Celtic_League "2001–02 Celtic League") until playoffs began in [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Celtic_League "2009–10 Celtic League").

When the UEFA Champions League reformatted in 1993, it added a "knockout stage" involving four teams that finished at the top two places in their respective groups. Like North American sports leagues, this setup prevented some participants from facing each other, necessitating a two-round knockout stage to determine the champions. It has since been expanded to the 4-round knockout stage today. The [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/Copa_Libertadores "Copa Libertadores") has applied a knockout stage since the 1988 tournament, expanding to the current four-round format next season. All intercontinental club football competitions now feature a knockout stage.

## Off-season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_(sports)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Off-season")]

"Offseason" redirects here. For other uses, see [Off Season](/wiki/Off_Season "Off Season").

The off-season, vacation time, or close season is the time of year when there is no official competition. During this time, events such as [drafts](/wiki/Draft_(sports) "Draft (sports)"), [transfers](/wiki/Transfer_(football) "Transfer (football)") and off-season [free agent](/wiki/Free_agent "Free agent") signings may occur. Generally, athletes continue training to some extent during the off-season in preparation for the next season with some leagues such as the NFL treating this as a major event with [training camp](/wiki/Training_camp "Training camp") being held in the preaseason.

Many player and coach retirements are often also announced during the offseason. And a number of teams often fire and hire new coaching and front office staff during the offseason period. Many sports [hall of fames](/wiki/Hall_of_fame "Hall of fame") also hold induction ceremonies for mostly former sportspeople during the offseason.

As most countries which have a league in a particular sport will operate their regular season at roughly the same time as the others, international tournaments may be arranged during the off season. For example, most European football league club competitions run from July or August to May, and so major international competitions such as the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") and [UEFA European Football Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Football_Championship "UEFA European Football Championship") are organised to occur in June and July.

In closed leagues [expansion](/wiki/Expansion_draft "Expansion draft") and [dispersal drafts](/wiki/Dispersal_draft "Dispersal draft") may happen in the offseason when new teams are added to the league or if existing teams fold during or in the aftermath of a season. In addition, league realignments and venues for Neutral site game venues may be announced also be announced in the offseason.

## Seasons by league

You can help by [editing the page](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Season_(sports) "Special:EditPage/Season (sports)") to add missing items, with references to [reliable sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources").

The table represents typical seasons for some leagues by month. Blank or white denotes off-season and pre-season months and solid colors mark the rest of the year. Leagues in the same sport use the same color.

* "**E**" denotes [exhibition](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game")/preseason games.
* "**Q**" denotes pre-competition qualifiers.
* "**S**" denotes the start of the regular-season.
* "**P**" denotes playoff(s)/postseason/knockout stages.
* "**F**" denotes Final(s).

1. **[^](#cite_ref-ACB_3-0)** A team from [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"), [BC Andorra](/wiki/BC_Andorra "BC Andorra"), has competed in the league since the [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_ACB_season "2014–15 ACB season").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-AFC_Champions_League_Elite_4-0)** Australia is a member of the AFC since 2006, and Israel was expelled from AFC in 1974.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-Campeonato_Brasileiro_Série_A_5-0)** Brazilian association football teams compete in state championships from January to March.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-ERCC_6-0)** Replaced the former European club competition, the Heineken Cup, effective in [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2014–15 European Rugby Champions Cup").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-European_Tour_season_7-0)** The current scheduling, in which the season starts in January, took effect in [2012](/wiki/2012_European_Tour "2012 European Tour"). In [2010](/wiki/2010_European_Tour "2010 European Tour") and [2011](/wiki/2011_European_Tour "2011 European Tour"), the schedule began late in the previous calendar year.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-European_Tour_geography_8-0)** The bulk of the tour's sole-sanctioned events are in Europe, with some in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") countries. It co-sanctions many other events with tours in Asia, Africa, and Australia. Along with the PGA Tour, the European Tour co-sanctions the [major championships](/wiki/Men%27s_major_golf_championships "Men's major golf championships") and the [World Golf Championships](/wiki/World_Golf_Championships "World Golf Championships"); one of the majors is held in the UK, one of the WGC events is held in China, and the rest of these events are in the US.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-IndyCar_9-0)** Since the 2015 season, the only series race held outside the U.S. is hosted by Canada. Australia, Brazil, and Japan have hosted races in the past.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-Liga_MX_10-0)** Like many football leagues in Latin America, Mexico divides its season into [two phases](/wiki/Apertura_and_Clausura "Apertura and Clausura") with separate champions in each phase. Unlike most leagues in that sport, Liga MX uses a knockout playoff, involving the top eight teams after league play, to determine its champions.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-Ligue1_11-0)** The current [2019–20 season](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Ligue_1 "2019–20 Ligue 1") features one team from [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco") in [AS Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC"), which has been in the league on numerous occasions, most recently since the [2013–14 season](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Ligue_1 "2013–14 Ligue 1").
10. **[^](#cite_ref-LMB_12-0)** The [Serie del Rey](/wiki/Serie_del_Rey "Serie del Rey") usually ends in September.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-LPGA_13-0)** Most LPGA events are held in the US, but it also sole-sanctions official money events in Thailand, Singapore, Mexico, China, Canada, Malaysia, and Taiwan, as well as an unofficial event in Brazil. Since 2013, two of its [majors](/wiki/Women%27s_major_golf_championships "Women's major golf championships"), [one](/wiki/Women%27s_British_Open "Women's British Open") in the UK and [the other](/wiki/The_Evian_Championship "The Evian Championship") in France, have been co-sanctioned with the [Ladies European Tour](/wiki/Ladies_European_Tour "Ladies European Tour"). The LPGA also co-sanctions events with the [Korean LPGA](/wiki/LPGA_of_Korea_Tour "LPGA of Korea Tour") and [Japan LPGA](/wiki/LPGA_of_Japan_Tour "LPGA of Japan Tour") in those countries and the [ALPG Tour](/wiki/ALPG_Tour "ALPG Tour") in Australia.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-MLB_14-0)** The [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") usually ends in late October, but can extend into early November.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-NASCAR_15-0)** Since the 2013 season, the top two NASCAR touring series, the [Cup Series](/wiki/Monster_Energy_NASCAR_Cup_Series "Monster Energy NASCAR Cup Series") and [Xfinity Series](/wiki/Xfinity_Series "Xfinity Series"), race only in the US. The third-level [Truck Series](/wiki/Gander_Outdoors_Truck_Series "Gander Outdoors Truck Series") has a race in Canada. The Xfinity Series has previously held races in Canada and Mexico. NASCAR also operates national series in [Canada](/wiki/NASCAR_Pinty%27s_Series "NASCAR Pinty's Series") and [Mexico](/wiki/NASCAR_Toyota_Series "NASCAR Toyota Series") and [a European series](/wiki/NASCAR_Whelen_Euroseries "NASCAR Whelen Euroseries"), plus many regional developmental series in the US.
14. **[^](#cite_ref-Chase_16-0)** NASCAR has used a playoff-style system to determine the champion of the Cup Series since 2004; in 2016, it extended this system to its other two U.S. national touring series. The playoffs in the Xfinity and Truck Series are shorter than that of the Cup Series.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-NFL_17-0)** The NFL currently takes at least one regular-season game [outside the US](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series") (London since 2007). The [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars") are nearing the end of an eight-year period (2013–2020) in which one home game is in London. The 2019 season is the fourth consecutive season for a scheduled game in Mexico City, though the game originally scheduled for Mexico City in 2018 was moved to Los Angeles. For several years in the early 21st century, the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") played [one home game per season in Toronto](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series").
16. **[^](#cite_ref-NHRA_18-0)** The [NHRA Mello Yello Drag Racing Series](/wiki/NHRA_Mello_Yello_Drag_Racing_Series "NHRA Mello Yello Drag Racing Series") uses a playoff system known as the *Countdown to the Championship* to determine the champion of each of the four drag racing classes.
17. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-NCAAF_19-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-NCAAF_19-1) The NCAA has never recognized [an official champion](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") in the top-level [Division I FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision"). Although the four-team [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff") began in 2014, it is not an official NCAA championship event. All other NCAA divisions determine officially recognized champions in playoff tournaments that start in November. The playoffs in Divisions [II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II_National_Football_Championship "NCAA Division II National Football Championship") and [III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III_National_Football_Championship "NCAA Division III National Football Championship") end in December. Since the 2010 season, the [Division I FCS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") [playoffs](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship "NCAA Division I Football Championship") end in January.
18. **[^](#cite_ref-NWSL_20-0)** The schedule listed here is that for the most recently completed 2018 season. In some years, the playoffs are held in October. In addition, the NWSL season is disrupted twice in a four-year cycle—first by the FIFA Women's World Cup, and then the following year by the Summer Olympics. In those years, fewer regular-season games are played, and the league takes a break for the stated event.
19. **[^](#cite_ref-PGA_21-0)** The vast majority of the PGA Tour's sole-sanctioned events are in the US proper, but the tour also has events in Malaysia, Mexico, Canada, and Puerto Rico. Along with the European Tour, the PGA Tour co-sanctions the [major championships](/wiki/Men%27s_major_golf_championships "Men's major golf championships") and the [World Golf Championships](/wiki/World_Golf_Championships "World Golf Championships"); one of the majors is held in the UK, one of the WGC events is held in China, and the rest of these events are in the US.
20. **[^](#cite_ref-PBA_22-0)** Calendar shown was for the 2011–12 season. It has been frequently adjusted since 2012–13 for multiple reasons, therefore the start and end of each conference and season varies from month to month.
21. **[^](#cite_ref-EPL_23-0)** While almost all teams in the multi-tiered [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system") are based in England, a small number of teams from Wales also take part. Of these, both [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") and [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_F.C. "Swansea City F.C.") have spent seasons in the Premier League.
22. **[^](#cite_ref-Primera_ARG_24-0)** The 2016–17 season marked the completion of Argentina's transition from its former calendar-year [Apertura and Clausura](/wiki/Apertura_and_Clausura_in_Argentine_football "Apertura and Clausura in Argentine football") season format to a single league season spanning two calendar years.
23. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Top14_25-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Top14_25-1) Depending on the calendar, the final may fall on the last weekend of May in some seasons.
24. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** Super League was founded in 1996 with teams in England and France; France had no presence in the league from 1998–2005.
25. **[^](#cite_ref-Supercars_27-0)** Currently, one race is held in New Zealand. Bahrain, China, the United Arab Emirates, and the USA have staged races in the past. Longer-term plans call for additional races in Hong Kong, India, the Philippines, Singapore, South Africa, and South Korea.
26. **[^](#cite_ref-Super_Rugby_29-0)** [Moana Pasifika](/wiki/Moana_Pasifika "Moana Pasifika") plays in New Zealand but represents Pacific Islanders in general.
27. **[^](#cite_ref-RWC1_30-0)** The playoffs, or "finals" as they are officially called, begin in late June in [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup") years.
28. **[^](#cite_ref-RWC2_31-0)** During World Cup years, the final match is held in July; in other years, the finals begin in July.
29. **[^](#cite_ref-RWC3_32-0)** In non-World Cup years, the final match is held in August.
30. **[^](#cite_ref-Celtic_League_33-0)** The league originally involved only the three Celtic nations; Italy joined beginning in the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Celtic_League "2010–11 Celtic League"), and South Africa in [2017–18](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Pro14 "2017–18 Pro14").
31. **[^](#cite_ref-oly_34-0)** During Summer Olympic years, the WNBA Finals run into late October. During years in which the FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup is held, the season is compressed, with the WNBA Finals ending in September. In other years, the WNBA Finals end in early October.

### Summary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_(sports)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Summary")]

| Sport | Duration |
| --- | --- |
| American football | Originally football was played only in the fall, but for many years the season has extended from late summer through early to mid-winter.  * The 17-game [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") regular season currently begins with [NFL Kickoff](/wiki/NFL_Kickoff "NFL Kickoff"), the first Thursday in September (after a month of exhibition games), and ends 18 weeks later around [New Year's Day](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Day "New Year's Day"). The playoffs culminate with the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") on the second Sunday in February. * The college season begins the last week in August. The regular season ends in late November, with playoff and [bowl games](/wiki/Bowl_games "Bowl games") throughout December and early January. * [Indoor football](/wiki/Indoor_American_football "Indoor American football"), past professional leagues such as the [USFL](/wiki/United_States_Football_League "United States Football League") and [XFL](/wiki/XFL_(2001) "XFL (2001)"), and some women's and amateur leagues play in the February to August season. |
| Association football | Usually August to May in the Northern Hemisphere, and February to November in the Southern Hemisphere. Exceptions are generally for one of two reasons:  * In some northern countries with severe winter weather (such as the United States, Canada, Finland, and Sweden), the season is contested within a calendar year (roughly March to November) to avoid the worst weather.   + However, other leagues use winter breaks to avoid most of the coldest days to prevent players suffering from hypothermia. * Some countries, primarily in Latin America, use a split season, known as [Apertura and Clausura](/wiki/Apertura_and_Clausura "Apertura and Clausura"). The traditional association football season from August to May is divided in two sections per season, each with its own champion. Apertura and Clausura are the Spanish words for "opening" and "closing". In French-speaking Haiti, these are known as the Ouverture and the Fermeture, while in English-speaking Belize, they are respectively the Opening and Closing seasons. The now-defunct second version of the North American Soccer League (NASL) adopted a split season in 2013; the season was divided into a Spring Championship and Fall Championship. * In some countries where soccer competes with locally more popular [football](/wiki/Football "Football") codes (i.e. Major League Soccer in the U.S., A-League in Australia), the season is arranged so as to minimize the time that it is in conflict with the more popular code(s). This arrangement also eases scheduling concerns regarding venues used for multiple football codes. However, in the case of the United States' MLS, their regular seasons can conflict with major tournaments such as the Gold Cup and FIFA World Cup due to the demand of the league's best national players to participate in the national squad.   (See [Domestic association football season](/wiki/Domestic_association_football_season "Domestic association football season") for details.) |
| Australian rules football | March to late August, with finals series extending up to late September or early October. |
| Baseball | Northern hemisphere summer leagues: March or April to early October, with playoffs extending up to early November. Northern hemisphere winter leagues: as the Australian Baseball League runs from November to early February, with playoffs extending up to late February. |
| Basketball | In most countries, late October to mid-April, with playoffs extending up to mid-June. The three major exceptions to this rule are:  * The U.S. college basketball season begins in mid-November. The regular season ends in the first days of March, followed by conference tournaments and then national championship tournaments that run into early April. * The Philippine Basketball Association has a unique calendar. Its season runs from varying periods since the 2012–13 season, and is divided into "conferences"—not the North American concept of subgroupings within a larger competition, but rather separate competitions involving the same set of teams, similar to football's [Apertura and Clausura](/wiki/Apertura_and_Clausura "Apertura and Clausura") in Latin America. Due to frequent adjustments to the calendar beginning in 2012, the months when each season begins and ends differ; it last ran from early October to August in 2011–12. The season was divided into three conferences from 1975 to 2003. It then transitioned to a two-conference season in 2004, and also changed to a season spanning two calendar years, with the transition completed in the 2004–05 season. The PBA returned to a three-conference season effective in 2010–11. The season starts with the Philippine Cup, restricted to Filipino players, followed by two other tournaments in which teams can field one non-Filipino, the Commissioner's and Governors' Cups. * The WNBA season is scheduled during the offseason of its parent league, the NBA. When the league was launched, all of its teams were owned by NBA teams and generally played in the same arenas as their NBA counterparts; however, this has changed over time. The scheduling also allows many WNBA players to participate in overseas leagues, primarily in Europe, Australia, and China, during the traditional basketball season. |
| Canadian football | July to late October, with playoffs extending into November. |
| Cricket | Year-round. Domestic seasons typically held in the driest period of the year—summer in temperate climates, dry season in tropical climates. |
| Golf | Year-round |
| Ice hockey | Early October to mid-April, with playoffs extending up to early June. The three major exceptions to this rule are:  * The U.S. men's college hockey season begins in early October. The regular season ends in late February or early March, followed by conference tournaments and then a national championship tournament that runs into mid-April. The women's season typically begins and ends two or three weeks earlier than the men's. * The KHL regular season begins in early September and ends in late February. The playoffs run from March to mid-April. * The Australian Ice Hockey League's season runs from April to mid-August, with playoffs extending up to late August. |
| Motor racing | Year-round, but generally concentrated from March to October. NASCAR runs from mid-February to late September, with playoffs extending up to late November. |
| Pesäpallo | The regular season begins on the first weekend of April. It is split at the 24-game mark, with the final regular season games taking place during the second week of August. The playoffs commence in the third week of August, and the finals are scheduled for the first week or second week of September. |
| Rugby league | Late February to October in both the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. |
| Rugby union | September to late May, sometimes the first weekend in June, in the Northern Hemisphere. In the Southern Hemisphere, Super Rugby starts in February and ends in early July in [World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup") years and mid-August in other years. Domestic competitions in [New Zealand](/wiki/Mitre_10_Cup "Mitre 10 Cup") and [South Africa](/wiki/Currie_Cup "Currie Cup") overlap slightly with the Super Rugby season, starting in July and ending in October or November. In [Australia](/wiki/National_Rugby_Championship "National Rugby Championship"), the domestic competition does not overlap at all with Super Rugby, instead beginning in August and ending in early November. |
| Swimming | Year-round |
| Tennis | Year-round |
